lines.text: This is a demo of the Smart Lab Digital Twin, a real-time digital twin of a laboratory room. A physics simulator models the room's temperature, humidity, and occupancy, then streams that telemetry over MQTT to a Streamlit dashboard and an interactive Three.js 3D view.

Crucially, this is a true digital twin, not just a digital shadow. Commands flow back from the dashboard to the simulator, closing the loop. And a PID controller automatically regulates the air conditioning power, to hold any target temperature.
Here is the live dashboard, in manual mode. The room is nearly empty and cool, around twenty-three degrees, with the air conditioning off and a twenty-four degree target.
Now we fill the lab and speed the simulation up. As occupancy climbs toward twenty people, their body heat pushes the temperature up quickly, toward the thirty-degree limit.
It overheats, past thirty-two degrees. So we manually switch the air conditioning on, and cooling jumps straight to one hundred percent power.
The room cools back down. As it approaches the twenty-four degree setpoint, the controller eases the power off smoothly, holding the target instead of crudely cycling on and off.
Now we hand over to auto mode. The system controls the air conditioning on its own, switching cooling on and off automatically as people enter and leave the room.
The twin reacts live. As we change the occupancy and raise the setpoint, auto mode adjusts the cooling power to match, with no manual input.

Finally, we lower the target to twenty and a half degrees. The command round-trips through MQTT, the simulator confirms it, and the AC ramps to full power to chase the new setpoint. A true closed-loop digital twin.
